outcomes.log: The ship revealed itself to the young world and watched its own arrival become the founding myth of another people. Whether gift or wound, it could never be taken back.

outcomes.label: Pass unseen
outcomes.description: Douse the flare, run dark, leave their sky unchanged.
outcomes.log: The ship ran dark past the young world and let it keep its innocent sky. Some aboard grieved the hands they did not extend; the council called restraint its own kind of gift.

title: The Silent Monuments
description: Drifting in the deep are the works of a people long gone — vast, patient structures of unknown purpose, older than the ship's whole line of ancestors. They could hold knowledge, danger, or only echoes.

outcomes.label: Send teams into the ruins
outcomes.description: Spend the time and the risk to learn what the builders left.
outcomes.log: The teams went into the silent monuments and came back awed, laden, and fewer. What they learned reshaped the ship's understanding of the dark; what took the lost, they never named.

outcomes.label: Record them and move on
outcomes.description: Scan from range. Some tombs are best left sealed.
outcomes.log: The ship mapped the monuments from a respectful distance and left them to their long silence. The archives gained a mystery and the crew kept its full number.

title: The Last Broadcast
description: A beacon drifts on the lane, still faithfully repeating a message in no known tongue after who knows how many centuries alone. Its makers are surely dust. The signal waits for an answer it was built to expect.

outcomes.label: Answer the beacon
outcomes.description: Send the reply it has waited eons for, wherever it goes.
outcomes.log: The ship broadcast an answer into the dark on the beacon's own frequency, completing a handshake begun before the founders were born. Nothing replied. It felt, somehow, like a kindness owed.

outcomes.label: Take it and go silent
outcomes.description: Recover the beacon's tech; leave no trace of ourselves.
outcomes.log: The salvage crews took the beacon apart for its patient, ancient tech and left the lane silent behind them. The message it had carried for eons ended, unanswered, in a cargo hold.

title: The Wandering Comet
description: A comet on a wild hyperbolic path will cross the ship's course exactly once, trailing volatiles and stranger compounds no survey has catalogued. It will not pass this way again in a hundred lifetimes.

outcomes.label: Match course and sample it
outcomes.description: Burn fuel to ride alongside and harvest what it carries.
outcomes.log: The ship chased the wandering comet down and rode its tail for a season, harvesting ice and rarer things. The tanks ran lower and the holds ran richer, and the comet fled on into the dark forever.

outcomes.label: Let it pass
outcomes.description: Log its path and keep the fuel for the mission.
outcomes.log: The astronomers tracked the comet's once-in-a-hundred-lifetimes pass and let it go untouched. Its data filled a page; its ices filled no hold.

title: The Great Bake-Off
description: A friendly rivalry between two galley decks over the ship's best ration-loaf has escalated into a full-blown culinary schism, complete with partisans, sabotage, and a contested trophy. The council is asked, with total seriousness, to adjudicate.

outcomes.label: Judge the contest
outcomes.description: Taste, deliberate, and crown a champion loaf.
outcomes.log: The council convened a solemn tasting and crowned a champion loaf to citywide acclaim and one deck's lasting grievance. Morale soared on a tide of bread.

outcomes.label: Declare it a tie
outcomes.description: Diplomatically insist both loaves are equally supreme.
outcomes.log: The council declared the bake-off a perfect tie, and both galley decks united in shared disappointment at the cowardice of it. Peace, of a grumbling sort, was kept.

title: The Second Departure
description: The first anomaly is surveyed and logged, and now the ship must do the hardest thing on a double charter — turn away from the direction of home and burn deeper out, toward a second objective a generation of the crew will not live to see completed.

outcomes.label: Hold to the full charter
outcomes.description: Both deeps, as contracted. Burn on toward the second.
outcomes.log: The ship turned its back on the direction of home and burned deeper out, honoring the whole charter. A generation swallowed its longing and flew on toward a horizon it would bequeath to its children.

outcomes.label: Skip the second deep, turn home
outcomes.description: One survey banked is enough. Take the partial pay and go.
outcomes.log: The council banked the first survey and turned for home rather than ask another lifetime of the crew. The pay would be partial; the relief in the corridors was total.

title: The Far Deep
description: The second anomaly is stranger than the first — and stranger than the charter promised. The survey teams argue whether what they are reading is a natural wonder or something that was made.

outcomes.label: Survey it to the bottom
outcomes.description: Spend the crew and the time to read the far deep entire.
outcomes.log: The teams read the far deep down to its strange foundations and lost a few of their number to its stranger depths. What they brought back would occupy the archives for centuries.

outcomes.label: Log the essentials and bank it
outcomes.description: Take the contracted readings and no more risk.
outcomes.log: The survey took its contracted readings of the far deep and asked no further questions of it. The charter was satisfied; the mystery was left, mostly, alone.

title: The Ascension Exodus
description: Centuries of augmentation have carried the Ascension Circle somewhere the rest of the ship cannot follow. They no longer eat at the common tables, no longer speak the common tongue without effort. Now they ask, gently, to be given a hull of their own and let go.

outcomes.label: Give them a lifeboat and let them go
outcomes.description: Part on good terms. They were always going to leave the human behind.
outcomes.log: The Ascension Circle took a lifeboat and their strange bright future and slipped away into the dark to become whatever they were becoming. The ship felt smaller, and a little more human, for their going.

outcomes.label: Refuse — one ship, one people
outcomes.description: Insist the augmented remain part of the whole, however strained.
outcomes.log: The council refused to let the Circle break away, and the augmented stayed — present, apart, and quietly counting the years until the question was asked again.

title: The Hardened Flame
description: The Keepers of the First Flame have spent generations turning the mission into scripture, and now their faith has teeth — they demand the whole ship keep their rites, or they will keep them alone, apart, behind a sealed bulkhead.

outcomes.label: Bend the ship to their rites
outcomes.description: Adopt the Keepers' observances shipwide to keep them in the fold.
outcomes.log: The ship took up the First Flame's rites to keep the peace, and the mission became, by degrees, a religion the whole crew practiced whether they believed it or not.

outcomes.label: Let them keep to themselves
outcomes.description: Give the orthodox their sealed quarter and let the schism harden.
outcomes.log: The Keepers sealed themselves behind their bulkhead and, over a generation, became a ship-within-the-ship that finally cut its own way loose. The First Flame took its scripture and its silence and left.

title: The Verdant Secession
description: The Verdant Kin have never loved the metal sky, and drift has only deepened their longing for real soil. When the route passes a habitable world, they ask — no, they announce — that they mean to go down and stay, and make a garden of it.

outcomes.label: Bless the planting and let them stay
outcomes.description: Send them down with seed and tools and a benediction.
outcomes.log: The Verdant Kin went down to the green world with seed and song, and the ship sailed on lighter and quieter, having planted a garden it would never see grow.

outcomes.label: Deny them — the mission needs their hands
outcomes.description: The ship cannot spare a whole people. Order them to stay.
outcomes.log: The council forbade the Kin their world, and they stayed — hands on the grow-decks, hearts on a planet dwindling astern. For a generation they tended the gardens like exiles.

title: The Failing Air
description: The habitat plant has been let go too long and now it is failing in earnest — scrubbers clogging, seals weeping, the air itself thinning deck by deck. This is the kind of neglect that empties a ship.

outcomes.label: All hands to the plant
outcomes.description: Stop everything. Throw the whole ship at the repair.
outcomes.log: Every hand that could hold a wrench turned to the failing plant, and the work went on around the clock until the air came back. The ship stopped breathing shallow and remembered how close it had come.

outcomes.label: Seal off the worst decks
outcomes.description: Abandon the failing sections; concentrate the good air.
outcomes.log: The council sealed the worst-hit decks and pulled everyone into the sound sections, tighter and grimmer than before. The air held for those who made the cut; the sealed corridors kept their silence.

title: The Open Locks
description: The security systems have decayed to the point of uselessness — locks that won't hold, sensors gone blind, blast doors frozen open. The ship is one bad actor away from a disaster it can no longer contain.

outcomes.label: Refit the security systems
outcomes.description: Spend the parts to make the doors and locks trustworthy again.
outcomes.log: The engineers rebuilt the locks and cleared the frozen doors until the ship could hold itself shut again. It felt, for the first time in a generation, genuinely secure.

outcomes.label: Post human watches instead
outcomes.description: No parts to spare — stand guards where the machines failed.
outcomes.log: With no parts to spare, the ship posted living watches at every failed door. The corridors were held by tired eyes and goodwill, and everyone understood it could not last.

title: The Seized Works
description: The engineering bay has worn down to the point where half its tooling is seized or broken, and the ship's whole capacity to make and mend is choking. Repairs elsewhere are backing up behind a workshop that can barely fix itself.

outcomes.label: Rebuild the workshop first
outcomes.description: Fix the thing that fixes everything, whatever it costs.
outcomes.log: The crew rebuilt the workshop before anything else, on the old principle that you mend the tools before the work. The backlog eased as the bay came back to life, and the ship could make things again.

outcomes.label: Improvise around it
outcomes.description: Limp on with hand-tools and ingenuity; save the resources.
outcomes.log: The machinists made do with hand-tools and stubbornness, and the ship kept flying on improvised fixes that mostly held. The bay decayed a little further; the crew got very, very good at making do.

title: The Shipborn Body
description: The physicians confirm what the tailors already knew: the generations born aboard are subtly, measurably different — longer-limbed in the low-spin decks, sharper-eyed in the dim, their bodies quietly reshaping to a world of metal and recycled air. The founders would not entirely recognize their descendants.

outcomes.label: Design the ship for who they are becoming
outcomes.description: Rebuild the decks around the shipborn body, not the founders'.
outcomes.log: The ship remade its handrails and doorways and gravity for the people it actually carried. The descendants moved through it like water; the old founder-proportions became a museum curiosity.

outcomes.label: Hold the founders' standard
outcomes.description: Keep the medical baselines the founders set. Resist the drift of the flesh.
outcomes.log: The medics fought the drift of the flesh with exercise regimes and gravity therapy, holding the descendants to the founders' shape at real cost. The old form endured, a little, by force of will.

title: The Recycled Palate
description: A generation has grown up whose bodies thrive on the ship's closed-loop diet in ways the founders' never could — and who find the 'real food' preserved in the founders' recipes faintly nauseating. The ancestral cuisine is becoming, to them, inedible history.

outcomes.label: Let the menu evolve
outcomes.description: Retire the founders' recipes. Feed the palate the ship actually has.
outcomes.log: The galleys retired the ancestral cookbook and cooked for the tongues aboard. The descendants ate happily; a few elders mourned flavors their grandchildren would never want.

outcomes.label: Preserve the ancestral cuisine
outcomes.description: Keep cooking the founders' food as heritage, whether they like it or not.
outcomes.log: The ship kept the founders' feasts on the calendar like sacraments, and the descendants dutifully choked down heritage once a year. The recipes survived; the appetite for them did not.

title: The Quiet Lungs
description: Deep adaptation has a hidden cost: the shipborn have grown so suited to the thin, still, perfectly regulated air that a sudden system fault leaves them gasping where the founders would merely have coughed. The very fitness for the ship has made the people fragile to its failures.

outcomes.label: Reintroduce hard conditions
outcomes.description: Deliberately vary the air and gravity to keep the people resilient.
outcomes.log: The ship began deliberately roughening its own perfect air, training resilience back into a people who had lost it to comfort. A few could not take the harder conditions; the rest grew hardy again.

outcomes.label: Make the systems failsafe instead
outcomes.description: Accept the fragility; engineer the ship to never, ever falter.
outcomes.log: Rather than toughen the people, the ship perfected the machines that kept them soft, layering failsafe on failsafe. The lungs stayed quiet and the whole survival of the line came to rest on the air plant never, ever stopping.

title: The Long Winter
description: Thin stores and a browning reactor at once — the galley cannot cook what the fields cannot grow, and the cold is creeping into the habitat decks. A ship can survive hunger or dark for a while, but rarely both together.

outcomes.label: Ration hard and huddle close
outcomes.description: Cut portions, concentrate warmth, and endure it as one.
outcomes.log: The ship pulled its people into the warm decks and thinned every plate, and the long winter took its toll in the old and the unlucky. Those who came through it came through it together.

outcomes.label: Burn the reserves to break it
outcomes.description: Spend everything at once to end the crisis fast, cost be damned.
outcomes.log: The council threw the whole reserve at the long winter and broke it in a season, leaving the coffers bare and the ship warm. It was reckless and it worked, which is its own kind of tradition now.

title: The Lean Pact
description: With the stores thin, the whole ship swears a rationing pact — equal shares, no exceptions, officers and infants alike. It is a hard, proud thing, and how it is remembered will shape the people for generations.

outcomes.label: Swear the pact ship-wide
outcomes.description: Equal shares for all, sealed by oath.
outcomes.log: The whole ship swore the lean pact, and for one hungry generation nobody ate a bite more than anyone else. It was remembered afterward as the year the crew became one people.

outcomes.label: Feed the essential crew first
outcomes.description: Keep the specialists strong; ration the rest harder.
outcomes.log: The council fed its specialists first and let the pact fail before it was sworn. The ship kept its experts sharp and its people divided into those who ate and those who watched.

title: The Pact Remembered
description: Generations after the lean years, the rationing pact has hardened into the ship's founding virtue — equal shares is not policy now but identity. A proposal to reward exceptional work with extra ration lands like heresy.

outcomes.label: Keep the pact sacred
outcomes.description: Equal shares forever. The pact is who we are.
outcomes.log: The ship reaffirmed the old pact as gospel, and equal shares became a thing beyond questioning. It bound the people tight and left no room for the ambitious, which most of them counted a fair trade.

outcomes.label: Allow earned rewards
outcomes.description: Let merit buy a little more. Loosen the old oath.
outcomes.log: The council let merit earn a larger plate, and the lean pact cracked at last into something more ordinary and less holy. The productive prospered; an old unity quietly dissolved.

title: The Trade Convoy
description: On the homeward leg the ship falls in with a trade convoy working the lane — they have fuel, parts, and fresh stock, and an appetite for the minerals and salvage filling the holds. The exchange rate is theirs to set.

outcomes.label: Trade cargo for provisions
outcomes.description: Sell minerals and salvage; buy fuel, parts, and food.
outcomes.log: The ship traded a hold of minerals to the convoy for full tanks and stocked lockers. The exchange favored the convoy, as it always does, and the homeward leg felt suddenly survivable.

outcomes.label: Keep the cargo for port
outcomes.description: The minerals are worth more sold at home. Decline.
outcomes.log: The council waved the convoy off and held its cargo for a better price at home. The holds stayed full and the lockers stayed thin, a gamble on making it back before either mattered.

title: The Drifting Tanker
description: A fuel tanker drifts dead on the lane, its crew long gone but its tanks — the sensors insist — still substantially full. Boarding a derelict is never free, but a low tank makes the risk look reasonable.

outcomes.label: Board and pump the tanks
outcomes.description: Cross to the derelict and take the reaction mass.
outcomes.log: The crews crossed to the dead tanker and pumped its tanks dry into their own. The ship came away fuelled for a decade and short a few who went aboard and did not radio back.

outcomes.label: Leave it drifting
outcomes.description: A dead ship's full tanks are a bad kind of luck. Pass it by.
outcomes.log: The navigators logged the drifting tanker and its too-convenient bounty and steered well clear. The old hands nodded; a full derelict is a story that rarely ends well.

title: The Sealed Deck
description: A bulkhead on a lower deck was welded shut before anyone now living was born. The founding log gives no reason. A maintenance sweep wants the space back; the elders want it left as it is.

outcomes.label: Cut it open
outcomes.description: Reclaim the volume and whatever the founders walled away with it.
outcomes.log: The seal came away in a gust of decade-stale air. Behind it: a mothballed workshop, a founder's tools laid out as if for a shift that never started. The deck breathes with the ship again, and the shops are the richer for the salvage.

outcomes.label: Leave it shut
outcomes.description: Some doors the founders closed on purpose. Keep it a closed door.
outcomes.log: The sweep was waved off. The sealed deck stays sealed, and the not-knowing becomes its own small faith — a thing the whole ship agrees not to touch, and is quietly closer for agreeing.

title: The Second Log
description: An archivist turns up a second ship's log, kept in parallel and never filed. Its account of the founding years diverges hard from the one the schools teach — messier, less proud, harder to love.

outcomes.label: Publish it
outcomes.description: Let the ship meet its truer, uglier history.
outcomes.log: The second log went into the archive beside the first, contradictions and all. The founders came down off their plinth a little; the record, for the first time in generations, is honest enough to be trusted.

outcomes.label: Bury it
outcomes.description: The clean story holds the ship together. Keep it clean.
outcomes.log: The second log went into a locked file, and the archivist was thanked for their discretion. The founding stays a story fit to raise children on — and somewhere a truer one waits, filed under nothing.

title: The Wandering Mind
description: The oldest system aboard — the founders' navigation archive — begins issuing course notes no one asked for. With the last engineers who understood it long dead, no one can say whether it is failing or thinking.

outcomes.label: Follow its course
outcomes.description: It has never steered you wrong. Let it steer.
outcomes.log: The correction shaved a decade's drift off the course and cost less mass than the navigators would have. It was right. No one can say how, and fewer each year even ask — the mind is trusted now the way weather is trusted.

outcomes.label: Wipe it, re-derive by hand
outcomes.description: Better a course you understand than one you obey.
outcomes.log: They purged the archive and re-derived the ship's course by hand from the founding tables — a year of drudgery and one bad wobble before the numbers came right. The crew understands its own heading again, and paid in sweat to.

title: The Festival War
description: Two decks' harvest festivals, drifted apart over generations, have landed in the same week, and each insists the other moved. The galley cannot cater both, and neither will yield the date.

outcomes.label: Let them compete
outcomes.description: Rival festivals, one week, may the best deck win.
outcomes.log: Both festivals ran, louder than ever out of pure spite, and the whole ship ate twice. Morale soared; the decks are not speaking, but they are not speaking very cheerfully.

outcomes.label: Fuse them into one
outcomes.description: One holiday, ship-wide, that nobody's grandparents ever kept.
outcomes.log: The council fused the two festivals into a single ship-wide holiday with a new name and older-sounding songs written last month. Within a generation no one will remember it was ever two — which is how all traditions start.

title: The Reconstructed Feast
description: The key spice for the founders' feast-day dish ran out lifetimes ago. A cook unveils a faithful reconstruction from ship stores. It tastes nothing like the archived recipe, and the mess deck has never been happier.

outcomes.label: Call it the real thing
outcomes.description: It is delicious. Let it be the feast.
outcomes.log: The reconstruction was declared the true feast-day dish by acclamation, the archive quietly overruled by the nose. The founders would not recognize a bite of it, and would, after one bite, forgive the ship entirely.

outcomes.label: Honor the archive
outcomes.description: Ration a substitute closer to what the founders ate.
outcomes.log: The archivists were consulted, the nearest substitute rationed out, and the feast served as the founders meant it — drier, greyer, correct. The old families were satisfied. Everyone else ate quietly and remembered last year's.

title: The Office of Lost Things
description: Without anyone ordering it, a generation has grown an elaborate bureau for cataloguing and returning lost tools and socks. It has forms now, a filing berth, and a proud staff of eleven.

outcomes.label: Make it real infrastructure
outcomes.description: Give the Office a budget and a mandate. Tools that stop vanishing are tools that stop being remade.
outcomes.log: The Office of Lost Things got a berth, a seal, and a line in the manifest. To general surprise it works — the shops stop bleeding tools, the fabricators run a shift lighter, and eleven people who were doing this anyway now do it on the record.

outcomes.label: Reassign the eleven
outcomes.description: Send the staff to real posts. The ship can find its own socks.
outcomes.log: The Office was thanked and disbanded, its eleven reassigned to posts that were genuinely short-handed. The filing berth is a store room again. People still turn up at the old hatch with lost spanners, and stand a moment, and go.

title: The Tow-Line
description: Decades into the haul, the main grapple anchor is fatiguing where the dead ship's mass drags hardest. The rig will not last the return as it is. Re-anchor it, or shed some of the prize to save the line.

outcomes.label: Re-rig the anchor
outcomes.description: Spend the parts and the shift-hours. Keep every tonne on the line.
outcomes.log: The riggers cut a new anchor bed into the spine and walked the tow-line across under load — a month of work no one wants to describe twice. The dead ship rides steady again, and the whole prize is still on the line.

outcomes.label: Shed part of the tow
outcomes.description: Cast off the outer holds. A lighter tow is a tow that gets home.
outcomes.log: They cut the derelict's outer holds loose and watched a fortune tumble away into the dark. The line runs easy now and the ship rides light — but the manifest they carry home is the poorer for it, and everyone did the arithmetic.

title: The Overloaded Decks
description: The tow draws parasitically on the ship — its cold holds kept barely warm, its archives kept dry, all off your grid and your air. The strain is telling on life support. Bear the load, or let the dead ship go cold.

outcomes.label: Bear the load
outcomes.description: Keep the prize whole. Carry its weight on your own systems.
outcomes.log: The council chose to carry the tow warm and whole, and the habitat plant ran a little harder for it every year of the haul. The archives will reach home readable. The scrubbers will need answering for.

outcomes.label: Let it go cold
outcomes.description: Cut the derelict off your grid. Save the ship; lose what freezes.
outcomes.log: They pulled the tow off the grid and let it freeze. The ship's air came easier at once — and somewhere in the dead ship's dark holds, seed-stock and film and things that do not survive the cold quietly stopped being cargo.

title: The Endless Haul
description: A generation has been born, come of age, and taken the line who have known nothing but the tow — the dead ship always behind, home always a century ahead. They ask the elders what the haul is for. The answer will shape who they become.

outcomes.label: Make the haul sacred
outcomes.description: The tow is the ship's great work. Let hauling it be who they are.
outcomes.log: The haul became the ship's religion — the tow-line watch its priesthood, the dead ship behind them a relic they were chosen to bear home. They are one people now, bound to a labor their grandparents took up. What they will do when the haul finally ends, no one has thought to ask.

outcomes.label: Point them at home
outcomes.description: Keep them dreaming of the planetfall none of them will live to see.
outcomes.log: The elders taught the new watch to work for the harbor at the end — a green world in the archives none of them would reach. It kept them steady and it kept them restless, a generation always half-turned toward a home that stayed a century off.

title: The Forge and the Garden
description: The Steel Covenant wants a mid-ship deck stripped and re-plated for a heavy foundry. The Verdant Kin have spent four generations turning that same deck into a living arboretum. Neither will yield it, and the quarrel has stopped being about the deck.

outcomes.label: Partition the deck between them
outcomes.description: Spend the council's credit brokering a line down the middle. Half foundry, half garden, and a shared grievance.
outcomes.log: The council ran a bulkhead down the contested deck — presses on one side, an arbor on the other, a sealed door between that both sides use and neither likes. The ship kept both its makers and its growers, and the credit it cost to keep them.

outcomes.label: Let the Covenant found its foundry-world
outcomes.description: The machinists have long wanted a rock of their own to build on. Let them take a lifeboat and go.
outcomes.log: The Steel Covenant took a lifeboat down to a bare metal world and stayed to build. The ship sailed on quieter and lighter — and found, a generation later, that the best of its machinists' craft had walked off the hull with them, and no one left aboard quite remembered how the hard repairs were done.

title: The Courts and the Creed
description: The Keepers of the First Flame have grown into the ship's moral authority, and they have run out of patience with the Meridian Accord's endless arbitration — courts that, they say, weigh every truth until it means nothing. They move to dissolve the Accord's standing tribunals.

outcomes.label: Keep the tribunals standing
outcomes.description: Spend the influence to defend the courts against the creed. A ship ruled by faith alone is a ship that stops asking questions.
outcomes.log: The council kept the Accord's tribunals open over the Flame's objection, and the ship stayed a place where a grievance could be weighed rather than judged. The Keepers withdrew, displeased but aboard, and the courts sat on.

outcomes.label: Let the creed rule
outcomes.description: Abolish the tribunals. The Accord will not serve a ship that has replaced its law with a fire.
outcomes.log: The tribunals were struck, and the Meridian Accord — arbiters with nothing left to arbitrate — asked for a hull and left rather than live under a law they could not question. The ship kept its creed and lost the quiet art of settling a quarrel before it drew blood.

title: The Cold Table
description: The Hearth Union's whole faith is the common table — that the ship eats as one people. But the Ascension Circle no longer eats, not as others do, and the Hearth has begun to wonder aloud what a table is for when a third of the ship will not sit at it.

outcomes.label: Set a place for those who cannot eat
outcomes.description: Spend the influence to keep the common table common — a seat kept even for the augmented who only sit, and do not eat.
outcomes.log: The Hearth kept its table whole, laying a place for the Circle who came, and sat, and did not eat, and were counted anyway. It cost the council to insist on it, and it held the ship together the way the table always had.

outcomes.label: Let the Hearth keep its own table
outcomes.description: Let the Union take a hull where the old rite still means something and everyone at the table still eats.
outcomes.log: The Hearth Union settled a warm green world and kept its table there, among people who all still ate at it. The ship that sailed on was more efficient and colder for their going — no one now whose whole calling was to make the ship feel like a home.

title: The Fire and the Field
description: The First Flame keeps a shrine-burn lit off the reactor — an eternal fire that has not gone dark since launch. The Verdant Kin, short of light for the grow-decks, have asked for that power. Two faiths, one reactor, and only so many megawatts.

outcomes.label: Ration the reactor to the gardens
outcomes.description: Dim the shrine-burn and give the surplus to the grow-decks. The faithful will grumble; the gardens will grow.
outcomes.log: The shrine-burn was banked to a low steady glow and its spared power run to the grow-decks. The Flame's faithful watched their eternal fire dim and said nothing kind, and the gardens, for the first time in a generation, had light enough to spare.

outcomes.label: Keep the eternal fire at full burn
outcomes.description: The flame has not gone dark since launch and will not now. The gardens will make do.
outcomes.log: The eternal fire kept its full burn, and the faithful were honored, and the grow-decks stayed dim and thin. The Kin logged the harvest shortfall against the shrine and did not forget it, and the ship ate a little less to keep a fire lit.

title: The Failing Ward
description: The medical bay has decayed past patching — the sterile fields flicker, the surgical suite can no longer be trusted to hold clean, the old diagnostic beds read false. It is becoming a place people go to and do not come back from.

outcomes.label: Strip the ship to rebuild the theatre
outcomes.description: Tear clean plate and working parts from wherever you can and make the ward whole again.
outcomes.log: They cannibalized three decks for clean plate and sound seals and rebuilt the surgical suite from the hull in. The ward holds sterile again, and the ship that gave up its spares to do it will feel the lack elsewhere for a generation.

outcomes.label: Fall back to triage medicine
outcomes.description: Do what can be done with clean hands and boiled water. Let the rest go.
outcomes.log: Medicine fell back to what a steady hand and boiled water could manage, and the ship relearned which illnesses it could no longer treat. The ward kept working, after a fashion, and the burial detail kept working too.

title: The Broken Beds
description: The hydroponics decks have gone too long unmended — bed liners split, nutrient loops crusted shut, the circulation pumps seizing one by one. The gardens are dying faster than they can be picked, and a dying garden is a starving ship a few years on.

outcomes.label: Rebuild the hydroponics
outcomes.description: Spend the parts and the power to re-lay the beds and re-seat the pumps. Modern farming, kept alive.
outcomes.log: The grow-decks were torn out and re-laid, pump by pump, liner by liner, until the water ran clean and green again. It cost the ship a fortune in parts and power, and it bought back the one thing the ship cannot buy: next year's food.

outcomes.label: Fall back to soil farming
outcomes.description: Abandon the failed hydroponics for low-tech soil beds. Hardier, hungrier on space, and a lean few years while it takes.
outcomes.log: The ship gave up on the high hydroponics and turned to soil — dirt beds in every spare corridor, a way of farming the founders had thought left behind. It will feed the ship in time, but the lean years between broke some health that the crowded, hungry wards could not mend.

title: The Crumbling Archive
description: It is not that the teachers have forgotten — it is that the archive itself is dying. The data-looms fail, the record substrate flakes and greys, whole shelves of the founding library reading back as noise. The ship's memory is rotting on its racks.

outcomes.label: Mount the great copying
outcomes.description: Set a generation to hand-copying the failing archive onto fresh media before it is lost. A vast, patient labor.
outcomes.log: A whole cohort gave its youth to the copying — reading the greying archive aloud and writing it fresh, shelf by failing shelf, before the noise took it. The library will outlive them now, in a hand not its own, and the ship kept its memory whole.

outcomes.label: Let the oldest records go
outcomes.description: Save what is used; let the deep archive — the parts no one has opened in lifetimes — fade.
outcomes.log: The council saved the working records and let the deep archive go — the founding debates, the old star-charts, the names of worlds no one was steering for anymore. The ship reads lighter now, and remembers less of why it ever left.

title: The Only World
description: A census officer brings the council a quiet finding: not one soul now aboard was alive at launch, and by the arithmetic of the voyage, not one now living expects to see the end. For everyone who breathes, the ship is not the way to the world. The ship is the world.

outcomes.label: Teach the voyage as a passage
outcomes.description: Hold the founding and the far harbor alive in every school. The ship is a corridor between two worlds, and these are the people of the crossing.
outcomes.log: The schools kept teaching the ship as a passage — a bridge thrown between a world left and a world promised, and every child a walker on it. It gave the voyage a spine and a sadness both: a people forever between, at home in neither dark they came from nor light they steer for.

outcomes.label: Teach the ship as home
outcomes.description: Let them have the ship as the whole and only world it has become for them. The mission can be an inheritance without being a homesickness.
outcomes.log: The council let the schools teach the ship as the world entire — not a corridor but a country, with its own weather of light-cycles and its own horizon of bulkheads. The people grew happy in it and strange to their charter, the far harbor fading into a story told at festivals, meaning less each time.

title: The Equidistant Dark
description: Deep in the middle of the crossing, no star near enough to grow or shrink, the long-range array shows a sky that does not change from watch to watch. A young officer reports, unsettled, that by every instrument the stars have stopped moving. They have not. It only looks that way from the exact middle of nowhere.

outcomes.label: Burn the beacons into the dark
outcomes.description: Run every running light and hail-beacon at full — a statement, to no one, that the ship is here and moving.
outcomes.log: The ship lit itself up against the unmoving dark and threw hails into a silence that would not answer for a thousand years. Nothing heard them. But the crew slept easier for a while, knowing their ship insisted, to the whole indifferent middle of nowhere, that it was here.

outcomes.label: Run dark and silent
outcomes.description: Douse the lights, save the power, and let the ship drift the deep middle unseen even by itself.
outcomes.log: The ship went dark to save its reactor for the far side, and the crew stood their watches by instrument alone through a sky that never moved. It was a grim, close time, and the ship came out of it thriftier and quieter and somehow more one thing than it went in.

title: The False Noon
description: The navigators announce the ship has passed the exact mathematical midpoint of the crossing — a moment that changes nothing, that no one can see, in a voyage with no days. A committee of the bored has proposed making a holiday of it. The council must rule on the calendar.

outcomes.label: Declare the Great Midpoint
outcomes.description: A blowout for the halfway mark — the biggest feast the ship has thrown since launch nobody remembers.
outcomes.log: The Great Midpoint was declared, and the ship threw itself a party for arriving at a mathematical point in the void that looked exactly like every other point. It was glorious. Within two generations children will believe it was always a holiday, which is the truest kind of tradition.

outcomes.label: A working toast and back to shift
outcomes.description: Mark it in the log, raise one cup on the mess deck, and keep the watch. Halfway is halfway.
outcomes.log: The midpoint got a line in the log and a single raised cup on each mess deck, and then the watch went back on. It was the sort of restraint the founders would have approved of, and the sort the party faction has not entirely forgiven.

title: The Castaways
description: A distress beacon resolves into a crippled boat — the last of some other venture's people, generations adrift on failing air, with a hold of preserved stores they can no longer eat fast enough to matter. They ask to be taken aboard. Every berth given is another mouth for the rest of the crossing.

outcomes.label: Take them aboard
outcomes.description: Open the locks. More hands, more mouths, and a debt of mercy the ship will carry — and feed — the rest of the way.
outcomes.log: The locks opened and the castaways came aboard, gaunt and grateful, and the ship was suddenly larger and hungrier and a little more crowded in every corridor. The galley redid its arithmetic for the rest of the crossing, and no one aboard regretted the choice, exactly, though everyone felt it at the table.

outcomes.label: Take the stores, not the people
outcomes.description: Trade for their preserved cargo and leave them their boat. The ship cannot feed everyone who is starving in the dark.
outcomes.log: The ship took the castaways' stores at a fair rate and left them their boat and their heading, and pulled away from the beacon before it stopped transmitting. The larder was fuller for it. The crew that made the trade did not talk about it much, then or after.

title: The Windfall Harvest
description: The garden world beneath is more generous than the survey promised — a growing season the ship could gorge on for a decade. But there is far more here than the holds can carry, and only so long before the ship must break orbit. A glut is its own kind of problem.

outcomes.label: Preserve it for the long haul
outcomes.description: Dry, salt, and vault everything the holds will take. The crews will relearn the old craft of putting food up against a lean century.
outcomes.log: The whole ship turned to the harvest — picking, drying, salting, vaulting — until the holds groaned full and the crews had, between them, remembered how a people feeds itself across a barren stretch. The larder will outlast the memory of this world, and the craft of filling it was worth as much as the food.

outcomes.label: Take what stores, sell the rest forward
outcomes.description: Load the holds and log the surplus as a manifest to trade at the next port of call. Food now, credits later, and none of it put up for a crisis.
outcomes.log: The ship took its fill and struck a forward contract on the rest — a manifest of the windfall to be redeemed at a port a century downstream. The holds are full enough and the writ is worth a good sum, and if a lean year comes before the port does, well, the ledger looked handsome at the time.

title: The Recycler's Gambit
description: Spare stock has run out, and the reclamation crews have made a proposal the council does not like: there are decks aboard that are not load-bearing — the old chapel, the founders' museum, the memorial arboretum — and they are full of metal and mass the ship could melt down and use.

outcomes.label: Feed the shrines to the foundry
outcomes.description: Strip the chapel, the museum, the memorial deck. They are only metal, and metal is what the ship is short of.
outcomes.log: The reclamation crews took the chapel first, then the museum, then the memorial arboretum where the founders' names were grown into the living wall. The ship ate its own shrines and made spars and fittings of them, and was sound of hull and poorer of soul, and did not entirely forgive the necessity.

outcomes.label: Hold the line, stay short
outcomes.description: Some mass is not for melting. Make do with less, and keep the places that make the ship more than a machine.
outcomes.log: The council refused the foundry its shrines, and the ship stayed short of parts and whole of heart, patching with wire and patience what it could not cast new. The reclamation crews grumbled at the waste of good metal, and the memorial arboretum kept the founders' names another generation.

title: The Ration Market
description: Rationing has done what rationing does: a quiet market has grown in the gaps of it, ration chits changing hands at three times face, the well-connected eating better than the fair share the council swore everyone would keep. The quartermasters want to know what the council intends to see.

outcomes.label: Break the market
outcomes.description: Raid the caches, void the hoarded chits, and make an example. Everyone back to the fair share, at the point of it if need be.
outcomes.log: Security walked the black market down in a week of hard searches and voided chits, and the fair share held again, resented and enforced. The ship ate equal and unhappy, and the ones who had been eating well remembered who had taken it from them.

outcomes.label: License it and tax it
outcomes.description: You cannot stop it, so make it pay. Legalize the trade, skim it for the common stores, and call the pragmatism a policy.
outcomes.log: The council licensed the ration market rather than fight it, taxed it for the common stores, and let a ship that was supposed to share alike learn to price its hunger instead. It kept the peace and paid a little into the tins, and it settled something about the ship that the founders had meant to be otherwise.

title: The Answer
description: Generations ago the ship painted an ancient dark mass with an active pulse — a thing old and not natural, left well astern. Now the long array wakes the watch: a signal has arrived on the exact frequency of that pulse, from dead ahead, weaker and slower and changed, as though the ship's own question has been all this time coming back an answer.

outcomes.label: Answer it
outcomes.description: The ship asked, once. Something answered. Reply, and learn what asks back across the dark.
outcomes.log: The ship replied to the thing that had replied to it, and spent a generation listening to the slow exchange sharpen — question, answer, question — with something that did not think in years. What was learned was worth a fortune to the archives and cost the crew their sleep, and the dark body passed into the ship's oldest ghost stories, verified.

outcomes.label: Never on that frequency again
outcomes.description: The founders should never have pinged it. Strike the frequency from the arrays, seal the old scan, and let the answer go unanswered forever.
outcomes.log: The council forbade the frequency, sealed the founders' scan under the oldest lock the archive had, and let the slow answer arrive to a ship that would not hear it. Whatever had been reaching back reached into silence, and the watch that made the choice slept better for a thing it chose not to know.

title: The Mirror
description: The forward array returns something impossible: a perfect echo of the ship itself, dead ahead and closing — same mass, same drive-signature, same running lights, as if an identical vessel were bearing down on a head-on course. The navigators are fairly sure it is the ship's own emissions, folded back by something massive between here and there. Fairly sure.

outcomes.label: Hail it
outcomes.description: Whatever is folding the ship's light back is worth understanding. Open a channel to the thing wearing the ship's face.
outcomes.log: The ship hailed its own reflection and mapped the lens that made it — a knot of bent space the charts had missed, worth every watt to have measured. For a week the crew watched their own hail come back a heartbeat late, mouthing their own words, and were glad when the lens fell astern.

outcomes.label: Log it and pass
outcomes.description: It is a lensing artifact and nothing more. Note the phenomenon, hold the course, and do not stare at it.
outcomes.log: The navigators logged the mirror as a gravitational artifact, held their heading straight through the ghost of their own ship, and did not dwell on how, for the span of the crossing, something ahead had matched them turn for turn.

title: The Rule Comes Due
description: A deck fire has filled the wards past what the medics can treat, and the cold rule the council wrote generations ago — the skilled saved first, the needed before the loved — is no longer a line in the archive. It is a list, and the medics are standing over it, waiting to be told whether the ship still means what it wrote.

outcomes.label: Apply the rule as written
outcomes.description: The ship decided this in cold blood generations ago so it would not have to decide it now. Honor the law. Save the needed.
outcomes.log: The medics worked the list as written, saving the skilled and the needed and passing by the rest, and the ship became, in one smoke-filled shift, exactly the machine its ancestors had coldly designed it to be. It survived intact and efficient, and something in it that had argued the rule was cruel stopped arguing.

outcomes.label: Break your own law
outcomes.description: Save whoever is nearest, skilled or not, needed or not. A law that can do this does not deserve to be kept.
outcomes.log: The medics threw the list away and saved whoever their hands reached first, and the ship broke its own oldest law in the smoke and did not regret it. It lost some it could not afford to lose and strained the wards to breaking, and learned that a rule written to be obeyed once can, once, be refused.

title: The Far Horizon
description: A generation has done the arithmetic that the charter tried to keep gentle: the ship is still outbound, still burning toward a target none of them will ever see resolve, and the survey their grandparents promised belongs to grandchildren not yet born. They are the crossing generation — all cost, no arrival. They want to know what they are for.

outcomes.label: Hold the heading, honor the crossing
outcomes.description: Teach the crossing itself as the work — that carrying the ship one more generation toward the horizon is a life's worth of purpose, arrival or no.
outcomes.log: The ship taught its crossing generation to take pride in the burn itself — in being the hands that carried her another lifetime toward a horizon they would never reach. It held them to the heading, and it asked of them a patience that is close kin to faith.

outcomes.label: Let the crossing become the point
outcomes.description: Stop pretending the destination is theirs. Let this generation build a life for its own sake in the long burn, target or no target.
outcomes.log: The council stopped asking the crossing generation to live for a horizon and let them live for the ship they were on. They grew content and a little untethered from the writ, and the far target became a thing the ship was pointed at rather than a thing anyone still ached to reach.

title: The Turnback Petition
description: Deep in the outbound push, with the target still a century off, a movement has gathered signatures the council cannot ignore: turn the ship around now, they say, and go home with nothing rather than spend three more generations reaching a structure that may be dust by the time anyone arrives. The charter, or the descendants. Choose.

outcomes.label: Press on — the charter stands
outcomes.description: The ship was cast off to reach the far target and it will reach it. Deny the petition and hold the burn.
outcomes.log: The council denied the petition and held the outbound burn, and the ship pressed on toward a horizon a hundred years off over the quiet fury of the ones who wanted to go home. The charter stood. So did the grudge, banked for the generations still owed to the crossing.

outcomes.label: Turn back now, with nothing
outcomes.description: Spare the descendants the rest of the crossing. Abandon the far target, lay in a course for home, and carry back only the ship and the people.
outcomes.log: The ship came about in the deep dark, a hundred years short of a target no living eye had ever seen, and laid in the long course for home with empty holds and lighter hearts. The charter was forfeit and the writ would pay nothing, and for the first time in three generations the crew was sailing toward a place someone aboard might actually live to reach.

title: The Aging Out
description: Three centuries into a one-way burn, the ship is doing something no ordinary voyage asks of it: growing old with nowhere to refit. A normal charter renews its worn systems at the destination; this one must nurse every module the whole way there, on nothing but what it carried and what it can strip from itself.

outcomes.label: Strip redundancy to keep the core fresh
outcomes.description: Feed the ship's spare systems to its essential ones. Arrive with no margin, but arrive with the vitals sound.
outcomes.log: The crews stripped the ship's every redundancy to keep the engineering bay and the habitat plant sound — arriving, eventually, with the vitals strong and nothing whatsoever held back against the next failure. It was the right call for a ship that could not refit, and it left her with no second chances in the hold.

outcomes.label: Let the margins fail, arrive threadbare
outcomes.description: Keep the reserves; let the non-essential systems wear out and go dark. The ship will reach the target worn thin but with its stores intact.
outcomes.log: The council chose to hoard its reserves and let the ship wear where it could stand to — darkened workshops, a hollowed watch, systems left to fail one by one across the long burn. The stores stayed full and the ship arrived threadbare, aged and thin, having spent its own condition to keep its margins.

title: The Green Hearth
description: Generations of shared work have blurred the old line between the Hearth Union and the Verdant Kin — the ones who keep the table and the ones who keep the garden. Their children intermarry, their festivals have merged, their councils sit together more often than apart. Now the two peoples ask the ship to recognize what has already happened: they wish to become one.

outcomes.label: Bless the union
outcomes.description: Let the table and the garden become one people. Two founding names become one living tradition.
outcomes.log: The Hearth and the Kin were joined by the ship's blessing, and where two peoples had kept the ship's warmth and the ship's green between them, now one people kept both. The founders' careful roster is one name shorter, and the ship is a warmer, less varied place for it.

outcomes.label: Keep the two names
outcomes.description: The founders set these peoples apart for a reason. Honor the old distinction, whatever the young are doing.
outcomes.log: The council held the two names apart as the founders drew them, and the Hearth and the Kin stayed, on the roster at least, two peoples. Their children went on marrying across the line and keeping one another's festivals, and calling the council's distinction a thing for the council.

title: The Technocrats' Compact
description: The Meridian Accord runs the ship's courts and the Steel Covenant runs its workshops, and after enough generations of running the ship's institutions shoulder to shoulder, the two have proposed something the founders never drew: a single administration, courts and forges under one hand, the argument and the labor finally in the same room.

outcomes.label: Form the compact
outcomes.description: One administration, courts and forges together. Efficient, unified, and a concentration of power the founders spread on purpose.
outcomes.log: The Accord and the Covenant folded into a single administration, and the ship ran, for a while, with a smoothness it had not known — the courts and the workshops speaking at last with one voice. The founders had kept those voices separate on purpose, and no one now aboard remembered quite why.

outcomes.label: Keep the powers apart
outcomes.description: Courts that judge the makers and makers who build for the courts — the founders split these on purpose. Refuse the merger.
outcomes.log: The council refused the compact and kept the courts and the forges as separate powers, each a check on the other, the way the founding charter drew them. It was less efficient and more free, and the two institutions went on eyeing each other across a line the founders had meant them to keep.
